name: Foam Roll Calves

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Tolerate pressure without excessive pain

steps: Sit on floor, place foam roller under one calf.
Cross opposite leg on top for added pressure (optional).
Roll from ankle to just below the knee — 30s per leg.
Pause on tender spots for 10-15s until tension releases.
formCues: ✅ Rotate leg to hit medial and lateral fibers
commonMistakes: ❌ Rolling directly over the Achilles tendon

breathing.pattern: Exhale on tender spots to release tension

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern — supine position supports spine.
injuryNotes.knee: Avoid rolling directly behind the knee joint (popliteal area).
injuryNotes.shoulder: Use hands for support — if shoulder is bothered, use elbows.

purpose: Releases overactive calves that restrict ankle dorsiflexion.
whyForYou: Tight calves restrict your ankle mobility, forcing compensations up the chain to your knee and back.
proTip: Use a lacrosse ball for deeper pressure on specific trigger points.

name: Foam Roll IT Band

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Tolerate pressure

steps: Side-lying, roller under outer thigh between hip and knee.
Support weight with bottom arm and top foot.
Roll slowly from hip to just above the knee — 30s.
Pause on tender spots for 10-15s.
formCues: ✅ Use top foot to control pressure
commonMistakes: ❌ Rolling directly on the knee joint

breathing.pattern: Exhale as you pause on tender areas

injuryNotes.lower_back: Side-lying may be uncomfortable — prop with pillows if needed.
injuryNotes.knee: Roll above the knee joint only — never on the joint itself. This exercise HELPS knee tracking.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Bottom arm supports weight — use elbow if wrist hurts.

purpose: Releases TFL/IT band tightness that contributes to knee valgus.
whyForYou: Tight IT band pulls on your left knee. Releasing it improves patellar tracking.
proTip: The IT band itself can't be stretched — you're really targeting the TFL at the top and the vastus lateralis underneath.

name: Foam Roll Adductors
steps: Lie face down. Place roller parallel to your body under one inner thigh.
Leg bent at 90° (frog position).
Roll from groin to just above the knee — 30s.
Pause on tender spots.
commonMistakes: ❌ Rolling too aggressively on inner thigh

injuryNotes.lower_back: Prone position — place pillow under hips if back is uncomfortable.
injuryNotes.knee: Avoid rolling directly at the knee joint line.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Arms support you — modify if shoulder hurts.

purpose: Releases overactive adductors that contribute to knee valgus and pelvic dysfunction.
whyForYou: Overactive adductors pull your knees in during squats. Releasing them protects your left knee.
proTip: Use a ball for deeper work on specific trigger points near the groin.

name: Foam Roll Lats
steps: Side-lying, roller under armpit area. Thumb pointing up.
Roll from armpit to mid-back — 30s per side.
Pause on tender spots.
Keep arm overhead for more stretch.
formCues: ✅ Roll slowly from armpit to mid-rib
commonMistakes: ❌ Rolling on the shoulder joint itself
❌ Not rotating enough to find the lat

breathing.pattern: Exhale to relax into tender spots

injuryNotes.lower_back: Side-lying is generally safe. No concern.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: IMPORTANT for your labrum — tight lats pull shoulder forward. Roll gently, avoid pressing into the joint itself.

purpose: Releases tight lats that pull the shoulder forward and contribute to impingement.
whyForYou: Tight lats contribute to your shoulder position issues. Releasing them takes stress off your labrum.
proTip: Place a lacrosse ball under the armpit for deeper teres major release.

name: Foam Roll Thoracic Spine
steps: Lie on roller positioned across upper back (NOT lower back).
Cross arms over chest or hold head.
Roll from mid-back to upper back — 30s total.
Extend over the roller at stiff segments for 2-3 breaths.
formCues: ✅ ONLY thoracic spine — never lower back
✅ Extend over roller at stiff spots
commonMistakes: ❌ Rolling the lower back (lumbar has no rib cage protection)

breathing.pattern: Exhale as you extend over the roller

injuryNotes.lower_back: NEVER roll the lower back — only thoracic. This actually HELPS your back by improving thoracic mobility so your lumbar doesn't compensate.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Helps shoulder mechanics by improving thoracic extension. Beneficial for labrum.

purpose: Restores thoracic extension and rotation — stiff T-spine forces lumbar and shoulder compensation.
whyForYou: Your thoracic mobility directly impacts both your lower back and shoulder. This is one of your most important prep exercises.
proTip: Place the roller at one segment, cross arms, and do 3 small crunches to mobilize that specific level.

name: Dead Bug (Bent Knee)

prerequisites.notes: Baseline core exercise — safe for all

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions pain-free, 10 reps each side with back flat

steps: Lie flat on back. Press lower back INTO the floor — no gap.
Arms straight up toward ceiling. Knees bent to 90°.
SLOWLY extend right arm + left leg. Back stays GLUED to floor.
Exhale 4s during extension. Pause 1s. Return. Switch sides.
formCues: ✅ Lower back NEVER leaves the floor
✅ Move slowly and controlled — this is a stability drill
commonMistakes: ❌ Back arching off floor (core lost tension)
coreBracing: MODERATE (40-50%) — press back into floor throughout.

breathing.pattern: Inhale to prepare, exhale during limb extension

injuryNotes.lower_back: If back lifts off floor at all, BEND the extending leg instead of straightening. This is your #1 core exercise — McGill-approved, spine-safe.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact — knees stay at 90°.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Keep extending arm within comfortable range. Don't force overhead if labrum is bothered.

purpose: Anti-extension core stability — the most important pattern for protecting a post-surgical spine.
whyForYou: Retrains your deconditioned deep stabilizers without any spinal load. Part of your McGill Big 3 protocol.
proTip: Place a towel under your lower back — if it slides out, you lost the brace.
source: McGill S. Low Back Disorders, NASM CPT 7th Edition

name: Plank (Knees to Full)

prerequisites.notes: Must demonstrate dead bug mastery before loading spine in plank

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Hold 30s from knees, 30s full plank pain-free

steps: Start on elbows and knees (regression) or elbows and toes (full).
Elbows directly under shoulders. Body forms straight line.
BRACE — squeeze glutes + abs simultaneously. Hold position.
Breathe normally — do NOT hold your breath.
formCues: ✅ Body forms straight line — head to heels
✅ Squeeze glutes to protect lower back

breathing.pattern: Continuous breathing — never hold breath during plank

injuryNotes.lower_back: Start from KNEES. If back sags or hurts, regress to dead bug. Never push through back pain in plank.
injuryNotes.knee: Knee version requires padding. Full plank has no knee stress.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Elbows under shoulders reduces labrum stress. If shoulder hurts, regress to dead bug.

purpose: Global core stability — challenges anti-extension under bodyweight load.
whyForYou: Progresses your dead bug to a loaded position. Builds the core endurance your back needs for daily protection.
proTip: Quality over duration. 15s with perfect form beats 60s with a sagging back.
source: McGill S. Low Back Disorders, NASM CPT 7th Edition

name: Side Plank (From Knees)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Hold 20s each side from knees pain-free

steps: Lie on side. Bottom elbow under shoulder, knees bent (regression) or straight (full).
Lift hips — body forms straight line from head to knees/feet.
HOLD — don't let hips drop or rotate.
Lower with control. Switch sides.

injuryNotes.lower_back: McGill Big 3 exercise — specifically targets lateral stability your spine needs. Start from knees.
injuryNotes.knee: Knee version — pad the bottom knee.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Bottom shoulder bears load — if LEFT side down bothers labrum, start with right side down only. Build up.

purpose: Anti-lateral flexion — critical for lateral spinal stability per McGill.
whyForYou: Part of your McGill Big 3. Lateral core stability prevents your back from compensating during single-leg and rotational movements.
proTip: Squeeze the top hip toward the ceiling for extra glute med activation.
source: McGill S. Low Back Disorders — Big 3

name: Bird Dog

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions pain-free, 8 reps each side with zero trunk rotation

steps: All fours — hands under shoulders, knees under hips.
Brace core gently. Extend RIGHT arm + LEFT leg simultaneously.
Hold 1s at full extension — body forms straight line.
Return slowly. Complete all reps one side, then switch.
formCues: ✅ Thumb points up on extending arm
coreBracing: MODERATE (30-40%) — enough to prevent trunk rotation.

breathing.pattern: Inhale to set, exhale during extension

injuryNotes.lower_back: McGill Big 3 — prescribed DAILY. If back fatigues, reduce reps not range. Stop if any radiating pain.
injuryNotes.knee: Pad knees if kneeling is uncomfortable.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Keep extending arm below shoulder height if labrum is aggravated.

purpose: Contralateral anti-rotation — trains spine to resist rotation under load.
whyForYou: Part of your mandatory McGill Big 3 daily protocol. Builds the anti-rotation stability your back needs.
proTip: Balance a water bottle on your lower back — if it falls, you're rotating.
source: McGill S. Low Back Disorders — Big 3

name: McGill Curl-Up

prerequisites.notes: McGill Big 3 — safe even for acute back pain

steps: Lie on back. ONE knee bent, one straight. Hands under lower back (natural curve).
Brace as if bracing for a punch. Do NOT flatten back.
Lift ONLY head and shoulders 1-2 inches off floor. Hold 5s.
Lower slowly. Do NOT curl up like a crunch — this is a BRACE, not a crunch.
formCues: ✅ Lift only 1-2 inches — not a crunch
commonMistakes: ❌ Curling up like a crunch (too much spinal flexion)
❌ Losing the brace during the hold
coreBracing: HIGH (60-70%) — brace before lifting, maintain throughout.

breathing.pattern: Breathe normally during the 5s hold — don't hold breath

injuryNotes.lower_back: McGill Big 3 — specifically designed for back patients. PRESERVES lumbar curve (hands underneath). This is NOT a crunch. Minimal spinal flexion.
injuryNotes.knee: One knee bent provides stability. No knee stress.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No shoulder involvement.

purpose: Spinal-safe core activation that preserves the neutral lumbar curve.
whyForYou: Completes your McGill Big 3 daily protocol. Activates rectus abdominis without dangerous spinal flexion.
proTip: Think 'brace and hover' — not 'curl up.' The lift is tiny. The brace is everything.
source: McGill S. Low Back Disorders — Big 3

name: Single-Leg Balance (Eyes Open)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 30s each leg eyes open without losing balance

steps: Stand near a wall or rack for safety.
Lift one foot off floor — hover it beside the ankle.
Balance for 20-30s. Focus on a fixed point ahead.
Switch legs. Progress by closing eyes once stable.
formCues: ✅ Soft knee on standing leg — not locked
✅ Tall posture — crown of head reaching up

injuryNotes.lower_back: Low risk. Stand tall.
injuryNotes.knee: If left knee hurts during single-leg stance, hold wall for partial support. Build up.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Foundation balance and proprioception — assesses and trains ankle/hip stability.
whyForYou: Exposes ankle and hip stability deficits that affect your knee tracking. Essential for sport performance.
proTip: Try eyes closed once you can hold 30s easily — doubles the challenge by removing visual input.

name: Single-Leg Balance with Reach

prerequisites.notes: Must master basic SL balance first

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 8 reaches each direction each leg without losing balance

steps: Stand on one leg. Arms at sides.
Reach free foot forward, to the side, and behind you — tap the floor.
Return to standing between each reach.
Complete all directions on one leg before switching.
formCues: ✅ Light tap only — don't transfer weight

breathing.pattern: Exhale during reach, inhale on return

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern — low load, great proprioception.
injuryNotes.knee: Start with small reaches on left side. Watch for valgus on the standing knee.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Dynamic balance — adds perturbation to single-leg stance for functional stability.
whyForYou: Challenges your balance in multiple planes — directly relevant to surfing, BJJ, and hiking stability.
proTip: Think of this as a modified Star Excursion Balance Test — a clinical assessment turned into training.

name: BOSU Ball Squat

prerequisites.notes: Must have basic balance and squat competency

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 12 reps stable on BOSU

steps: Stand on flat side of BOSU (dome down). Feet hip-width.
Arms forward for counterbalance. Find your balance.
Squat slowly to comfortable depth — 4s descent.
Hold 2s at bottom. Drive up through heels — 1s.
commonMistakes: ❌ Going too deep before mastering stability
❌ Rushing — this is a stabilization drill

breathing.pattern: Inhale on descent, exhale on ascent

injuryNotes.lower_back: Unstable surface increases core demand. If back complains, regress to floor squat.
injuryNotes.knee: Limited depth — stay within comfortable ROM. If left knee is unstable on BOSU, use floor version.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Squat pattern on unstable surface for Phase 1 neuromuscular challenge.
whyForYou: Challenges your proprioception while building squat mechanics — relevant for surfing balance.
proTip: Start by just standing on the BOSU for 30s before adding squats.

name: Stability Ball Wall Squat

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 15 reps pain-free with good form

steps: Place ball between lower back and wall. Feet shoulder-width, slightly forward.
Squat slowly — 4s descent. Ball rolls with you.
Descend to comfortable depth (thighs parallel or less). Hold 2s.
Drive up through heels — 1s ascent.
commonMistakes: ❌ Feet too close to wall (knees go past toes)
coreBracing: MODERATE (30-40%) — ball assists but core still works.

breathing.pattern: Inhale on descent, exhale on ascent

injuryNotes.lower_back: Ball supports your back — safest squat variation. Excellent starting point.
injuryNotes.knee: Control depth carefully. For left knee, stop at 45° initially. Feet forward reduces knee stress.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Wall-assisted squat learning pattern — ball supports back while teaching squat mechanics.
whyForYou: Safest way to learn the squat pattern. Ball supports your post-surgical back while you build leg strength.
proTip: Move feet further from wall to shift more load to glutes and less to quads.

name: Glute Bridge (Bodyweight)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions, 15 reps pain-free with glute-dominant activation

steps: Lie on back. Knees bent, feet hip-width, heels near glutes.
SQUEEZE glutes first — then push through HEELS to lift hips.
Rise until body forms straight line. HOLD 2s with hard glute squeeze.
Lower slowly — 2s descent.
commonMistakes: ❌ Feeling it in lower back = STOP (glutes aren't firing)
coreBracing: MODERATE (30-40%) — maintain neutral spine at top.

breathing.pattern: Inhale at bottom, exhale on lift, brief hold at top

injuryNotes.lower_back: If you feel this in your back instead of glutes, STOP. Reduce height, squeeze glutes harder before lifting. This exercise HELPS your back.
injuryNotes.knee: Adjust foot position until comfortable. Wider feet may be easier on left knee.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact — lying position.

purpose: Activates dormant glutes — when glutes don't fire, the back compensates.
whyForYou: Post-surgical back = inhibited glutes. This wakes them up and breaks the back-compensation cycle.
proTip: Place a fist between knees and squeeze — turbocharges glute med activation.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition, McGill S. Low Back Disorders

name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions, 10 reps each side pain-free, pelvis level

steps: Set up as bilateral bridge. Extend one leg straight up or hold knee to chest.
SQUEEZE glute of working leg. Push through heel to lift hips.
Hold 2s at top. Pelvis stays LEVEL — no rotation.
Lower slowly. Complete all reps one side before switching.
formCues: ✅ Pelvis stays LEVEL — no hip drop

injuryNotes.lower_back: If pelvis rotates or back takes over, regress to bilateral bridge. Single-leg demands more stability.
injuryNotes.knee: Non-working leg position matters — choose whatever is comfortable for left knee.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Unilateral hinge — exposes and corrects side-to-side glute strength imbalances.
whyForYou: Forces each glute to work independently. Reveals which side is weaker (likely left after surgery).
proTip: Place hands on hip bones to feel if pelvis stays level. If one side drops, that glute is weaker.

name: Romanian Deadlift (Dumbbell)

prerequisites.notes: Must demonstrate hip hinge competency with bodyweight first

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions pain-free, flat back maintained throughout, RPE 6

steps: Stand with dumbbells at thighs, feet hip-width. Slight knee bend.
HINGE at hips — push butt BACK. DBs slide down thighs.
Lower until hamstring stretch (mid-shin). Back stays FLAT.
SQUEEZE glutes to stand. Drive hips forward.
formCues: ✅ Hinge from HIPS — not rounding back

breathing.pattern: Inhale on descent, exhale on ascent

injuryNotes.lower_back: Start VERY light — groove the pattern first. Back must stay FLAT throughout. Any rounding = stop. No radiating symptoms. This is your home-workout hinge.
injuryNotes.knee: Slight knee bend protects joint. Not a deep squat pattern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Just holding DBs — no stress.

purpose: Hip hinge with dumbbells — builds posterior chain strength in a controlled pattern.
whyForYou: Your home-workout alternative to the trap bar deadlift. Same pattern, lighter load, accessible equipment.
proTip: Imagine closing a car door with your butt — that's the hip hinge.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition, NSCA Essentials of Strength Training

name: Trap Bar Deadlift (High Handles)

prerequisites.notes: Must master DB RDL first. Back severity must be ≤3.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions pain-free at RPE 7, perfect form confirmed

steps: Step INSIDE trap bar. Grab HIGH handles. Set back FLAT.
BRACE hard — full Valsalva. Push the FLOOR away.
Hips drive FORWARD to lockout. Stand tall.
REBRACE before every rep. Lower controlled — 3s descent.
formCues: ✅ Flat back — chest up, lats engaged
✅ Push floor away — leg drive, not back pull
✅ HIGH handles ONLY until cleared for low
commonMistakes: ❌ Jerking the bar off the floor
coreBracing: MAXIMUM (90-100%) — full Valsalva brace, rebrace every rep.

breathing.pattern: Big breath at bottom, brace, lift, exhale at top

injuryNotes.lower_back: HIGH handles ONLY until capacity increases. NO conventional deadlift — trap bar only. Any tingling, numbness, or radiating pain = STOP IMMEDIATELY. Stay under 70% 1RM.
injuryNotes.knee: Watch for valgus. VMO pre-sets mandatory before this lift.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Neutral grip is shoulder-safe — no concerns.

purpose: King compound lift. Trap bar reduces lumbar shear ~20% vs straight bar.
whyForYou: The only approved deadlift variation for your back. High handles reduce ROM to stay within tolerance.
proTip: Flat shoes or barefoot. Imagine pushing the earth away from you.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition, Swinton PA et al. (2011) trap bar biomechanics

name: Trap Bar Deadlift (Low Handles)

prerequisites.notes: Must master high handles. Back severity must be ≤2.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: High handles mastered for 4+ sessions at RPE 7, back severity ≤2

steps: Same as high handles but grab LOW handles — increased ROM.
Deeper hip position required. Back must be even more rigid.
BRACE maximally. Drive floor away. Lockout fully.
This is an ADVANCED progression — earned, not given.
formCues: ✅ Extra ROM demands better hip mobility
✅ Only when cleared by progression criteria
commonMistakes: ❌ Same weight as high handles (reduce load)
coreBracing: MAXIMUM (90-100%) — even more critical with increased ROM.

injuryNotes.lower_back: BLOCKED for you currently (severity 3 > gate of 2). This is a future goal once back severity drops to ≤2. Requires PT clearance.
injuryNotes.knee: Greater ROM demands more knee flexion. Must be pain-free.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact — neutral grip.

purpose: Full ROM trap bar deadlift — advanced progression when back tolerates increased range.
whyForYou: A future goal. Currently blocked by your back severity. Something to work toward as you progress through phases.
proTip: Dropping from high to low handles is like a whole new exercise — reduce weight 20-30% when transitioning.
source: NSCA Essentials of Strength Training, Swinton PA et al.

name: Wall Push-Up

prerequisites.notes: Easiest push — start here if pressing hurts

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 20 reps pain-free with full ROM

steps: Stand arm's length from wall. Hands shoulder-width on wall.
Lean in — lower chest toward wall. 4s descent.
Touch chest to wall. Push back to start — 1s.
Keep body straight — don't bend at hips.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Very low spinal load. Safe.
injuryNotes.knee: Standing — no knee stress.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Lightest pressing load possible. If shoulder pinches even here, consult PT before any pressing. ER warm-up still mandatory.

purpose: Entry-level horizontal push — builds pressing capacity from zero.
whyForYou: Starting point for rebuilding pressing strength. Low enough load to test your shoulder's tolerance before progressing.
proTip: The further your feet are from the wall, the harder it gets. Start close, move back over weeks.

name: Incline Push-Up

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 15 reps pain-free with full ROM

steps: Hands on bench/elevated surface, shoulder-width. Body straight.
Lower chest toward surface — 4s controlled descent.
Brief pause at bottom. Push up — protract at top.
Keep core tight throughout — no sagging.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Keep core braced — no sagging hips.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Higher incline = less load. Start with counter height and progress lower. ER warm-up mandatory.

purpose: Progressive push-up — adjustable difficulty by changing surface height.
whyForYou: Bridge between wall and floor push-ups. Adjustable to your shoulder's daily tolerance.
proTip: Counter → bench → low box → floor. Progress down over weeks, not days.

name: Floor Push-Up

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 10 reps with full ROM and no shoulder pain

steps: Hands shoulder-width on floor. Full plank position.
Lower chest to floor — 4s controlled descent. Elbows at 45°.
Chest touches or hovers 1 inch. Push up explosively.
Protract shoulder blades at top.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Core must be braced. Sagging = back stress. Regress if needed.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Full ROM may stress labrum. If any pinching, regress to incline. ER warm-up mandatory.

purpose: Standard push-up — bodyweight horizontal push benchmark.
whyForYou: A milestone in your pressing progression. Shows your shoulder can tolerate full bodyweight pressing.
proTip: Place a tennis ball under your chest as a depth target — consistent range every rep.

name: Landmine Press

prerequisites.notes: Core stability basic + shoulder cleared for pressing. Safest loaded press for labrum.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions pain-free at RPE 7

steps: Staggered stance. Hold barbell end at shoulder height.
BRACE core. Press UP and forward along the arc.
Protract shoulder blade at top — push through.
Return slowly under control.
coreBracing: MOD-HIGH (60-70%) — prevent rotation and back arch.

breathing.pattern: Exhale on press, inhale on return

injuryNotes.lower_back: Brace hard. Staggered stance reduces spinal load.
injuryNotes.knee: Staggered stance — front knee tracks straight.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Your SAFEST pressing option. Arcing path avoids end-range shoulder danger. ER warm-up MANDATORY before this exercise. Any click or pinch = reduce weight.

purpose: Safest pressing pattern for labrum tears — arcing path avoids end-range danger.
whyForYou: Your labrum tear prohibits overhead pressing at end-range. This is the safe alternative that builds real pressing strength.
proTip: Think 'punching the ceiling at an angle' — not pressing straight up.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition, Shoulder rehab literature

name: Dumbbell Floor Press

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions pain-free at RPE 7, full floor press ROM

steps: Lie on floor, knees bent. Dumbbells at chest height.
Press up — NEUTRAL grip (palms facing each other).
Lower until elbows TOUCH floor — brief pause.
Press back up. Pause eliminates stretch reflex.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Floor supports spine. Keep back flat.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Floor limits ROM to protect labrum. Use NEUTRAL grip only. BLOCKED if shoulder severity >2. ER warm-up mandatory.

purpose: Pressing with floor-limited ROM — protects shoulder by preventing elbows from going too deep.
whyForYou: Floor limits range to protect your labrum. Builds true pressing strength without the risky end-range position.
proTip: The floor pause kills momentum — builds honest pressing strength.

name: Dumbbell Bench Press

prerequisites.notes: Floor press mastered. Shoulder severity must be ≤1 for full bench ROM.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Floor press mastered, shoulder severity ≤1, PT clearance for full ROM pressing

steps: Lie on bench, feet flat. DBs at chest, neutral or pronated grip.
Press up — bring DBs together at top.
Lower controlled until elbows at 90° or slightly below.
Press back up. Maintain arch and shoulder blade retraction.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Bench supports back. Slight arch is OK.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: BLOCKED for you currently (severity 2 > gate of 1). Future goal when labrum severity drops to 1. Full bench ROM is riskier than floor press.

purpose: Full ROM dumbbell pressing — advanced horizontal push.
whyForYou: A future milestone. Currently blocked by shoulder severity. Demonstrates full shoulder recovery when you reach it.
proTip: Use neutral grip (palms facing) for maximum shoulder safety even when cleared.

name: Band Pull-Apart

prerequisites.notes: No prerequisites — safe for all levels

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 20 reps pain-free with full retraction

steps: Hold band at shoulder height, arms straight, shoulder-width grip.
Pull band APART — squeeze shoulder blades together.
HOLD 1s at full retraction. Return slowly.
Light band — this is high-rep, low-load.
formCues: ✅ Chest opens — not just arm movement
commonMistakes: ❌ Band too heavy — should be easy

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern — standing, light load.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Excellent for labrum — builds rear delt to balance the joint. Light band. Should never cause pain.

purpose: Rear delt and scapular retraction — counterbalances all pressing and forward posture.
whyForYou: Home/outdoor alternative to face pulls. Same shoulder-protective benefit, zero equipment needed beyond a band.
proTip: Do 20 reps between every set of pressing — your shoulders will thank you.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition, Shoulder rehab literature

name: Chest-Supported Dumbbell Row

prerequisites.notes: Back-safe — no spinal loading. No prerequisites needed.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions, 12 reps with full retraction pain-free

steps: Lie face down on incline bench. Arms hang straight down.
RETRACT shoulder blades first — then PULL elbows to hips.
SQUEEZE shoulder blades 1s at top. Lower slowly.
Keep chest on pad throughout.
formCues: ✅ Retract THEN pull — blade squeeze initiates
commonMistakes: ❌ Pulling with biceps instead of back

breathing.pattern: Exhale on pull, inhale on lower

injuryNotes.lower_back: SAFEST row for your back — ZERO spinal load. The bench stabilizes everything. Your go-to back exercise.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Use NEUTRAL grip to protect labrum. Avoid pronated grip.

purpose: Back thickness with zero spinal loading — bench does the stabilizing.
whyForYou: The safest rowing variation for your post-surgical back. Bench eliminates spinal load completely.
proTip: Think 'elbows to back pockets' — cues the lats over the biceps.

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions pain-free, 10 reps each side no trunk rotation

steps: One hand and knee on bench. Opposite foot on floor. Back FLAT.
Let DB hang. Shoulder relaxed.
RETRACT shoulder blade. PULL elbow to hip — squeeze 1s.
Lower slowly — full stretch at bottom.
commonMistakes: ❌ Rotating torso to get weight up

injuryNotes.lower_back: Bench support reduces spinal load. Keep back FLAT — rounding = reduce weight.
injuryNotes.knee: Pad kneeling knee on bench.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Neutral grip. Don't force stretch at bottom if labrum is sensitive.

purpose: Unilateral back development — bench support reduces spinal load.
whyForYou: Hand/knee support on bench takes stress off your back while building pulling strength.
proTip: Think 'start the lawn mower' — drive elbow back, not just pull with hand.

name: Cable Row (Seated)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions, 12 reps with upright torso maintained

steps: Sit with chest pad (if available). Feet on platform, knees slightly bent.
Grab V-bar or neutral grip handle. Sit tall.
PULL handle to lower chest — squeeze shoulder blades 1s.
Return slowly — don't round forward.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Minimal forward lean. Do NOT round lower back. Use chest pad if available.
injuryNotes.knee: Slight knee bend — don't lock.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Neutral grip safest for labrum. Avoid wide/pronated grip.

purpose: Horizontal pull with controlled load — constant cable tension throughout ROM.
whyForYou: Seated position with optional pad limits spinal load while training your pulling pattern.
proTip: Imagine squeezing a tennis ball between your shoulder blades at peak contraction.

name: Inverted Row

prerequisites.notes: Must master DB row and have core stability

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions, 10 reps with body at 45° or lower

steps: Set bar at waist height in rack (or use TRX). Grip overhand or neutral.
Hang underneath — body straight, heels on floor.
PULL chest to bar — squeeze shoulder blades hard.
Lower controlled. Body stays rigid like a reverse plank.
formCues: ✅ Pull chest to bar, not chin

injuryNotes.lower_back: Body must stay rigid. If back sags, raise the bar higher or use feet closer.
injuryNotes.knee: No direct impact. Can bend knees to reduce difficulty.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Can use neutral grip for shoulder safety. If shoulder pinches, regress to DB row.

purpose: Bodyweight horizontal pull — scalable by changing body angle.
whyForYou: Builds pulling strength using just bodyweight — great for home and outdoor training.
proTip: Higher bar = easier. Lower bar = harder. Feet elevated on bench = hardest.

name: Bodyweight Squat

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 15 reps with good form, knees tracking, chest up

steps: Feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out. Arms forward for balance.
SIT DOWN AND BACK — hips go back first, then knees bend.
Descend to comfortable depth — 4s controlled. Chest stays tall.
Drive through heels to stand — 1s ascent.

breathing.pattern: Inhale on descent, exhale on ascent

injuryNotes.lower_back: Keep chest tall to minimize forward lean and back stress.
injuryNotes.knee: Depth limited to comfort on left side. Watch for valgus. VMO pre-activation mandatory.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Foundation squat pattern — assesses and builds bilateral lower body strength.
whyForYou: Establishes your squat mechanics before adding load. Essential movement pattern for all sports.
proTip: Hold a doorframe and squat — removes balance challenge to learn the pattern first.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition — Movement Pattern Development

name: Goblet Squat

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions, 12 reps pain-free with knees tracking

steps: Hold DB/KB at chest with both hands. Elbows under the weight.
Feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out. Brace core.
SIT DOWN AND BACK — 4s descent. Elbows between knees at bottom.
Drive through heels. Stay upright.
formCues: ✅ Chest tall — weight counterbalances you upright

injuryNotes.lower_back: Front load naturally reduces spinal shear. Stay UPRIGHT. Light weight Phase 1.
injuryNotes.knee: Depth limited to comfort. VMO pre-activation mandatory. Watch valgus on left.
injuryNotes.shoulder: If left shoulder can't hold goblet position, reduce weight or use BW squat.

purpose: Front-loaded squat that teaches upright torso — best squat pattern teacher.
whyForYou: The front load counterbalances you upright, reducing back stress. Teaches excellent squat mechanics.
proTip: Use elbows to push knees out at the bottom — opens the squat.

name: Bulgarian Split Squat (Bodyweight)

prerequisites.notes: Must have single-leg stability and goblet squat mastery

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions, 10 reps each side bodyweight with good tracking

steps: Rear foot on bench, laces down. Front foot positioned forward.
DESCEND straight down — 3s controlled.
DRIVE through front HEEL to stand. Torso upright.
Complete all reps one side, then switch.
commonMistakes: ❌ Rear foot doing too much work

injuryNotes.lower_back: Upright torso minimizes spinal load. Hold rack for balance.
injuryNotes.knee: Start BODYWEIGHT ONLY on left side. Watch for valgus. Depth limited. VMO pre-activation mandatory. Knee severity must be ≤2.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact — bodyweight only.

purpose: Unilateral squat — forces each leg to work independently, correcting imbalances.
whyForYou: Exposes and corrects left-right imbalances from your knee surgery. Hold a rack for balance.
proTip: Hold a rack for balance until the pattern is owned. Depth comes later.

name: Bulgarian Split Squat (Loaded)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions, 10 reps each side loaded with good tracking

steps: Same setup as bodyweight version. Hold dumbbells at sides.
DESCEND controlled — 2s. Front knee tracks over toes.
Drive through front heel. Torso stays upright.
Complete all reps one side before switching.
formCues: ✅ Same form as BW — load doesn't change mechanics
✅ Dumbbells at sides, not on shoulders
✅ Control the weight, don't let it control you
commonMistakes: ❌ Adding weight before BW is mastered

injuryNotes.lower_back: DBs at sides (not on shoulders) reduce spinal load. Stay upright.
injuryNotes.knee: If valgus increases under load, reduce weight. Left side may need lighter load than right.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Holding DBs at sides — if grip irritates left shoulder, reduce weight.

purpose: Loaded unilateral squat — builds serious single-leg strength.
whyForYou: Once BW is mastered, adding load builds the unilateral strength you need for sports.
proTip: Start with 5-lb DBs. Adding even a little weight is a big jump in single-leg difficulty.

name: Front Squat

prerequisites.notes: Advanced — requires barbell competency, all injury severities ≤2

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Goblet squat + loaded BSS mastered, all severities ≤2, PT clearance

steps: Bar in front rack position — elbows HIGH, bar on deltoids.
Feet shoulder-width. Brace HARD.
Descend — stay UPRIGHT. Elbows stay high throughout.
Drive through heels. Fight to keep elbows up.
formCues: ✅ Upright torso — more than back squat
commonMistakes: ❌ Wrist pain from poor rack position
coreBracing: MAXIMUM (90-100%) — full brace with axial load.

breathing.pattern: Big breath, brace, descend, exhale at top

injuryNotes.lower_back: BLOCKED for you currently (severity 3 > gate of 2). Axial loading requires back severity ≤2 and Phase 3+. Future goal.
injuryNotes.knee: BLOCKED currently (severity 2 = gate of 2, borderline). Requires full pain-free goblet squat depth first.
injuryNotes.shoulder: BLOCKED currently (severity 2 = gate of 2, borderline). Front rack position stresses wrist and shoulder. Requires full flexibility.

purpose: Advanced bilateral squat — forces the most upright torso of any barbell squat.
whyForYou: A long-term goal. Currently blocked by multiple injury severities. Shows full recovery when accessible.
proTip: Cross-arm grip is an option if front rack mobility is limited. But work on wrist/shoulder flexibility first.

name: Face Pulls

prerequisites.notes: No prerequisites — mandatory for shoulder health

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 15 reps with full external rotation at end range

steps: Cable at HIGH position. Grab rope overhand.
Pull elbows BACK and APART — bring rope to face.
SQUEEZE rear delts 1s — finish in 'double bicep pose'.
Return slowly.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Light load — no spinal concern.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: This exercise HELPS your labrum. MANDATORY counterbalance to all pressing. Every pressing session.

purpose: Most important shoulder corrective — counterbalances pressing and forward posture.
whyForYou: MANDATORY every session you press. Builds the rear delt and rotator cuff strength your labrum needs.
proTip: Finish each rep in 'double bicep pose' — external rotation at top maximizes RC benefit.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition, Shoulder rehab literature

name: Banded External Rotation

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 12 reps pain-free each side

steps: Pin elbow to side at 90° bend.
Rotate forearm outward ~45°.
Return SLOWLY. Start LEFT side (weaker).

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: MANDATORY before any pressing session. This HELPS the labrum. Front pinch = reduce range.

purpose: Pre-activates rotator cuff before pressing — protects the shoulder joint.
whyForYou: Your labrum tear requires RC activation before every pressing movement. Non-negotiable warm-up.
proTip: Pin a rolled towel between elbow and ribs for perfect form.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition, Shoulder rehab protocols

name: Dumbbell Bicep Curl
steps: Stand tall, DBs at sides, palms forward.
Curl both up — elbows PINNED to sides.
Squeeze at top. Lower slowly — 2s.
Full extension at bottom.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No swinging — lean against wall if needed.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Neutral grip (hammer curl) if supinated grip bothers labrum.

purpose: Elbow flexion isolation — supports pulling and grip.
whyForYou: Supports your rowing and deadlift grip. Light isolation aids connective tissue health.
proTip: Stand against a wall — if back leaves wall, you're cheating.

name: Tricep Pushdown (Cable/Band)
steps: Face cable machine, grab rope or bar at chest height.
Pin elbows to sides. Push handle DOWN to full extension.
SQUEEZE triceps at bottom. Return slowly.
Elbows don't move — only forearms.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern — standing isolation.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Pin elbows. If overhead position of start bothers shoulder, use shorter cable/lower starting position.

purpose: Tricep isolation — supports pressing movements.
whyForYou: Supports your landmine press and push-up pressing. Builds lockout strength.
proTip: Rope attachment lets you pull apart at bottom for extra contraction.

name: Lateral Raise (Dumbbell)

prerequisites.notes: Shoulder severity ≤2. Must tolerate 90° abduction pain-free.

steps: Stand tall, light DBs at sides. Slight elbow bend.
Raise arms to sides — STOP at shoulder height (90°).
Hold 1s. Lower slowly.
Lead with pinkies slightly for external rotation.

injuryNotes.lower_back: No concern.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: STOP at 90° — going higher risks impingement with labrum tear. Severity must be ≤2. If any pinching, stop immediately. Light weight only.

purpose: Medial deltoid isolation — builds shoulder width and cap.
whyForYou: Phase 2+ exercise for shoulder aesthetics. Only when your labrum tolerates it.
proTip: Think 'pouring water from a pitcher' — slight internal rotation at 90° recruits medial delt.

name: Standing Calf Raise

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 15 reps full ROM

steps: Stand on step edge — heels hanging off. Hold wall for balance.
Rise onto toes — SQUEEZE calves. Hold 1s.
Lower slowly below step — full stretch. 2s descent.
Full ROM — below step to full rise.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Low load — no concern.
injuryNotes.knee: Straight knee targets gastroc. If knee complains, do seated (bent knee = soleus).
injuryNotes.shoulder: Hold rail — no stress.

purpose: Calf strength and ankle stability.
whyForYou: Strong calves support your ankle stability and reduce compensations to your knee and back.
proTip: Single-leg version exposes imbalances. Weak side gets extra reps.

name: VMO Wall Sit

prerequisites.notes: Knee rehab baseline — safe for all

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 20s hold pain-free at 45°

steps: Back flat on wall. Slide to ~45° knee bend.
Focus on inner quad (VMO). Actively flex it.
Hold 20s. Breathe continuously.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Back flat on wall — supported. No concern.
injuryNotes.knee: 45° MAX — never 90°. Joint pain = too deep. VMO activation is MANDATORY before any squatting.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Isolates VMO — the #1 patellar tracking protector.
whyForYou: Left VMO is weaker after knee surgery. Must pre-activate before every squatting pattern.
proTip: Hand on inner quad — feel it contract. Can't feel it = too deep.

name: Terminal Knee Extension (Band)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 15 reps with full lockout, VMO visibly contracting

steps: Anchor band behind knee at knee height. Face anchor.
Start with slight knee bend (~20°).
EXTEND knee fully — lock out. Squeeze VMO 1s.
Return slowly to slight bend. Repeat.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Standing — no spinal load.
injuryNotes.knee: Terminal extension specifically targets VMO in the range most important for patellar tracking. Pain-free only.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact.

purpose: Targets VMO in the terminal range of knee extension — most critical range for patellar tracking.
whyForYou: Directly strengthens the VMO in the exact range your left knee needs most for stability.
proTip: Place hand on inner quad above the kneecap — feel the VMO pop at full extension.

name: Pallof Press (Tall Kneeling)

prerequisites.notes: Bird dog mastered first for anti-rotation baseline

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions, 10 reps each side without any rotation

steps: Tall kneeling perpendicular to cable. Handle at chest.
BRACE. Press handle straight out. DON'T rotate.
HOLD 2s at extension. Resist the pull.
Return slowly. Complete all reps, switch sides.

breathing.pattern: Exhale during press out, hold at extension

injuryNotes.lower_back: One of the BEST exercises for your back. Anti-rotation trains exactly what your spine needs.
injuryNotes.knee: Kneeling — pad knees. If left knee can't kneel, do standing version instead.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Arms at chest height — no overhead.

purpose: Anti-rotation core stability — kneeling removes leg assistance.
whyForYou: Your spine needs anti-rotation stability more than any crunching movement. This delivers it.
proTip: Squeeze the glutes of both legs while kneeling — locks the pelvis for maximum core challenge.

name: Pallof Press (Standing)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions, 10 reps each side standing

steps: Stand perpendicular to cable. Feet hip-width.
Handle at chest. BRACE. Press straight out.
HOLD 2s at extension. No rotation.
Return slowly. Switch sides.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Excellent for your back. Anti-rotation is exactly what your spine needs.
injuryNotes.knee: Standing with soft knees — no concern.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Chest height — no overhead.

purpose: Anti-rotation standing — more functional than kneeling.
whyForYou: Progresses your anti-rotation training to a standing position — closer to real-world demands.
proTip: Try it during your sport — imagine resisting rotation while surfing or in BJJ guard.

name: Pallof Press (Split Stance)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions, 10 reps each stance

steps: Split stance perpendicular to cable. Inside foot forward.
Handle at chest. Press out — resist rotation.
Narrower base = harder. HOLD 2s at extension.
Switch stance AND sides.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Split stance increases demand — back severity must be ≤3.
injuryNotes.knee: Front knee soft. Adjust stance if left knee is uncomfortable.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No overhead.

purpose: Anti-rotation with reduced base of support — most demanding Pallof variation.
whyForYou: Split stance mirrors athletic positions — directly transfers to your sport performance.
proTip: The inside foot forward makes it sport-specific for cutting and rotation resistance.

name: Ab Wheel Rollout (From Knees)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Plank 60s solid. Phase 2+.

steps: Kneel with wheel in front. Hands on handles.
BRACE hard. Roll forward SLOWLY — maintaining flat back.
Extend as far as possible while keeping back FLAT.
Pull back using core — not hip flexors.
formCues: ✅ Roll only as far as you can control

breathing.pattern: Exhale on rollout, inhale on return

injuryNotes.lower_back: BLOCKED for you currently (severity 3 > gate of 2). Advanced exercise. Only when back severity improves.
injuryNotes.knee: Kneel on pad.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Overhead position at full extension — shoulder must tolerate this.

purpose: Advanced anti-extension — highest core demand in the anti-extension family.
whyForYou: A future progression once your back and shoulder severities improve. Something to work toward.
proTip: Use a stability ball first — same movement, more forgiving. Ball to ab wheel to standing.
source: McGill S. Low Back Disorders — Advanced Progressions

name: Single-Arm Farmer Carry

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 3 sessions, 30s each side without lateral lean

steps: Hold ONE DB/KB in one hand at your side.
Stand TALL — don't lean toward or away from weight.
Walk 30s. Perfectly upright — as if holding nothing.
Switch hands. Repeat.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Excellent lateral core stability exercise. Start VERY light — any lean = too heavy.
injuryNotes.knee: Flat surface. Short steps.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Alternate hands. Light weight.

purpose: Anti-lateral flexion carry — trains core to resist side-bending under load.
whyForYou: Walking under asymmetric load builds the lateral stability your back needs. Functional rehab.
proTip: Check in a mirror — you should look identical to walking without weight.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition — Carry Progressions, McGill

name: Suitcase Carry

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 30s each side, heavier load, no lean

steps: Hold heavier DB in one hand — suitcase style.
Walk 30s. Body stays PERFECTLY vertical.
Resist all lateral bending. Shoulders stay level.
Switch hands. Repeat.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Heavier version of SA farmer. Must be able to stay perfectly vertical.
injuryNotes.knee: No direct impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Alternate sides evenly.

purpose: Heavy anti-lateral flexion carry — progressed SA farmer carry.
whyForYou: Heavier lateral challenge. Builds the core endurance your back needs for all daily activities.
proTip: This is one of the most functional exercises in existence — you literally carry things every day.
source: McGill S. Low Back Disorders, NSCA

name: Prone Lying (Passive Extension)

prerequisites.notes: Step 1 of McKenzie — safe even in acute pain

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Tolerate prone lying for 5 min without increased peripheral symptoms

steps: Lie face down on a firm surface. Arms at sides.
Allow your back to sag naturally into extension.
Breathe deeply and relax completely — let gravity work.
Hold for 5 minutes. Monitor for symptom centralization.
formCues: ✅ Monitor symptoms — should centralize (move toward spine)
commonMistakes: ❌ Continuing if symptoms peripheralize (move to legs)
❌ Using a soft surface (firm is essential)

injuryNotes.lower_back: McKenzie Step 1. First assessment position. Symptoms should CENTRALIZE (move toward midline). If they PERIPHERALIZE (spread to legs), STOP and consult PT. Safe even in acute disc herniation as a test.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Arms at sides or under forehead. Adjust for comfort.

purpose: McKenzie Step 1 — passive lumbar extension to assess directional preference and begin centralization.
whyForYou: Starting point for your daily McKenzie protocol. Tests whether extension helps your back (it should for disc issues).
proTip: Do this FIRST thing in the morning before any other movement — sets your disc position for the day.
source: McKenzie R. Treat Your Own Back, StatPearls NBK539720
